title: NCLEX-RN Prep Center
description: Aggregated clinical judgment practice across ADN curriculum

Cardiovascular.question: Left-sided heart failure — which finding is most consistent?
Cardiovascular.rationale: Left-sided HF reduces LV output, causing blood to back up into pulmonary circulation — crackles, orthopnea, pink frothy sputum, and S₃ gallop. JVD and peripheral edema suggest right-sided failure.
Cardiovascular.clinical_judgment_focus: Differentiate left- versus right-sided failure by identifying where congestion backs up — lungs vs. systemic circulation.
Cardiovascular.clinical_why: Differentiating left vs right HF guides diuretic use, positioning (high Fowler's), and monitoring (daily weights, I&O).

Cardiovascular.question: Most important warfarin teaching?
Cardiovascular.rationale: Warfarin inhibits vitamin K-dependent clotting factors — consistent dietary vitamin K prevents INR swings; teach bleeding precautions and avoid NSAIDs without provider approval.
Cardiovascular.clinical_judgment_focus: Patient education on warfarin must address dietary consistency, not just medication timing.
Cardiovascular.clinical_why: Supratherapeutic INR causes GI and intracranial bleeding — subtherapeutic INR fails anticoagulation.

Cardiovascular.question: Which shock type presents with warm flushed skin early then hypotension?
Cardiovascular.rationale: Septic (distributive) shock causes massive vasodilation, producing warm, flushed skin in early phases. As shock progresses, compensatory mechanisms fail and hypotension develops despite warm extremities initially. Cardiogenic shock presents with cool, clammy skin; hypovolemic shock shows pale, diaphoretic extremities.
Cardiovascular.clinical_judgment_focus: Early septic shock may look 'warm' — do not be reassured by flushed skin in a febrile patient.

Cardiovascular.question: Refractory hypotension in septic shock after fluids — next:
Cardiovascular.rationale: When septic shock remains hypotensive after adequate fluid resuscitation, vasopressor support is indicated. Norepinephrine is often first-line to achieve MAP ≥65 mmHg and restore organ perfusion. Additional fluid boluses alone without vasopressors in refractory shock delay critical perfusion support.
Cardiovascular.clinical_judgment_focus: Escalate from fluids to vasopressors when septic shock does not respond to volume resuscitation.

Cardiovascular.question: Daily weight gain of 2 lb in CHF signals:
Cardiovascular.rationale: Daily weight is the most sensitive early indicator of fluid retention in heart failure — 2 lb gain in 24 hours signals 1 liter of retained fluid. Patients should weigh daily at the same time; report gains of 2–3 lb in a day or 5 lb in a week. Catching fluid retention early prevents hospitalization for acute decompensated HF.
Cardiovascular.clinical_judgment_focus: Daily weights catch fluid retention before crackles and dyspnea develop.

Cardiovascular.question: Orthostatic hypotension is defined as:
options: SBP drop ≥20 mmHg or DBP ≥10 mmHg with symptoms on standing
Any BP change lying to standing
Cardiovascular.rationale: Orthostatic hypotension is defined as a drop in SBP ≥20 mmHg or DBP ≥10 mmHg within 3 minutes of standing, often with symptoms. This indicates inadequate cerebral perfusion on position change and increases fall risk. Any BP change or HR increase alone does not meet the clinical definition.
Cardiovascular.clinical_judgment_focus: Orthostatic vital signs detect volume depletion and autonomic dysfunction before falls occur.

Cardiovascular.question: The prefix 'brady-' in bradycardia means:
Cardiovascular.rationale: The prefix 'brady-' means slow — bradycardia is a resting heart rate below 60 bpm in adults. Symptomatic bradycardia (hypotension, altered mental status, chest pain) may require atropine per ACLS protocol. Not all bradycardia requires treatment; athletes may have normal resting rates below 60.
Cardiovascular.clinical_judgment_focus: Medical terminology decoding supports accurate communication and safer care.

Respiratory.question: Appropriate SpO2 target for COPD on supplemental O2?
Respiratory.rationale: COPD patients are chronic CO₂ retainers; hypoxic drive maintains ventilation. Target SpO₂ 88–92% to prevent CO₂ narcosis while treating exacerbation.
Respiratory.clinical_judgment_focus: Balance oxygenation with ventilation risk — chronic retainers need lower SpO2 targets than acute patients.
Respiratory.clinical_why: Titrate O₂ to lowest saturation meeting target — ABG confirms pH and PaCO₂ trends.

Respiratory.question: Asthma with silent chest and exhaustion indicates?
Respiratory.rationale: Silent chest in asthma means minimal air movement despite obstruction — exhaustion and impending respiratory failure, not improvement. Prepare epinephrine, assisted ventilation.
Respiratory.clinical_judgment_focus: Silent chest in asthma is a danger sign — do not interpret absence of wheeze as improvement.
Respiratory.clinical_why: Wheezing may decrease as patient fatigues — worsening obstruction, not resolution.

Respiratory.question: COPD chronic baseline may include:
Respiratory.rationale: Chronic COPD patients retain CO2 and compensate with renal bicarbonate retention, producing compensated respiratory acidosis. Their baseline PaCO2 is elevated with near-normal pH — this is their compensated 'normal.' Over-oxygenating these patients can shift this fragile balance and cause acute CO2 narcosis.
Respiratory.clinical_judgment_focus: Know the compensated baseline in COPD — their 'abnormal' ABG may be their normal.

Respiratory.question: ARDS P/F ratio Berlin definition uses PEEP:
Respiratory.rationale: Berlin ARDS criteria require bilateral infiltrates, P/F ratio <300, and PEEP ≥5 cm H2O not fully explained by cardiac failure. ARDS severity is classified by P/F ratio: mild 200–300, moderate 100–200, severe <100. Lung-protective ventilation with low tidal volumes (6 mL/kg) is the cornerstone of management.
Respiratory.clinical_judgment_focus: ARDS requires lung-protective strategies — understand Berlin criteria for severity.

Respiratory.question: Which finding requires immediate notification?
options: SpO₂ 88% with RR 32 and accessory use
Respiratory.rationale: SpO2 88% with respiratory rate 32 and accessory muscle use indicates acute respiratory distress requiring immediate action. The nurse must assess the airway, apply oxygen, position the patient, and notify the provider promptly. Mildly elevated BP, moderate pain, and normal temperature are not acute threats to airway or breathing.
Respiratory.clinical_judgment_focus: Accessory muscle use with hypoxia signals respiratory failure — act on breathing before other findings.

Neurological.question: Suspected stroke within 45 minutes — first priority?
Neurological.rationale: Stroke protocol: establish last known well, ABCs, IV access, blood glucose (hypoglycemia mimics stroke), CT head, then consider tPA if ischemic and within window.
Neurological.clinical_judgment_focus: Time-critical stroke care still requires glucose verification and imaging before thrombolytics.
Neurological.clinical_why: Giving tPA before ruling out hemorrhage or hypoglycemia causes catastrophic harm.

Neurological.question: SCI T4: pounding headache, BP 210/118 — first action?
Neurological.rationale: Autonomic dysreflexia (T6 and above): pounding headache, hypertension, bradycardia — sit upright, loosen constrictive clothing, identify trigger (full bladder, fecal impaction), notify provider.
Neurological.clinical_judgment_focus: Autonomic dysreflexia in SCI above T6 requires immediate upright positioning and trigger removal.
Neurological.clinical_why: BP can exceed 300 mmHg causing stroke — never place flat (increases cerebral perfusion pressure).

Neurological.question: Before thrombolytics in suspected stroke:
options: CT head to rule out hemorrhage
Neurological.rationale: Thrombolytics (tPA) are contraindicated in hemorrhagic stroke — they would worsen bleeding catastrophically. Non-contrast CT head is the emergent imaging study to rule out hemorrhage before thrombolytic consideration. Time is critical, but ruling out bleed takes priority over administering tPA.
Neurological.clinical_judgment_focus: Rule out hemorrhagic stroke before thrombolytics — CT is non-negotiable.

Neurological.question: FAST assessment includes:
Neurological.rationale: FAST is the stroke screening mnemonic: Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call emergency services. Rapid recognition and activation of the stroke team maximizes the window for thrombolytic therapy. Early identification directly impacts functional outcomes and mortality.
Neurological.clinical_judgment_focus: FAST enables community and bedside stroke recognition — time activation saves brain tissue.

Gastrointestinal.question: Cirrhosis with confusion and asterixis — priority intervention?
Gastrointestinal.rationale: Hepatic encephalopathy from ammonia accumulation — lactulose acidifies colon, traps ammonia, increases bowel movements. Protein restriction is temporary, not permanent.
Gastrointestinal.clinical_judgment_focus: Connect neuro changes in liver disease to ammonia accumulation and initiate targeted treatment.
Gastrointestinal.clinical_why: Asterixis and confusion can progress to coma — monitor mental status and hold sedatives that worsen encephalopathy.

Gastrointestinal.question: Acute pancreatitis initial management?
Gastrointestinal.rationale: Acute pancreatitis: NPO to rest pancreas, aggressive IV fluids (third-spacing), pain control, monitor for ARDS and hypocalcemia. Oral intake resumes when lipase trends down and pain resolves.
Gastrointestinal.clinical_judgment_focus: Bowel rest and hydration are foundational in acute pancreatitis before nutrition is reintroduced.
Gastrointestinal.clinical_why: Early feeding in severe pancreatitis can worsen inflammation — follow enteral protocol when stable.

Gastrointestinal.question: Best technique order for abdominal assessment?
Gastrointestinal.rationale: Abdominal assessment follows the order: inspect, auscultate, percuss, then palpate (IAPPP). Auscultation must occur before palpation because palpation can alter bowel sounds and lead to inaccurate findings. Palpating before auscultating is a common error that compromises assessment accuracy.
Gastrointestinal.clinical_judgment_focus: Assessment technique order prevents altering the very findings you need to detect.

Gastrointestinal.question: Bowel sounds absent for 5 minutes in a post-op abdomen may indicate:
options: Ileus or obstruction — correlate with nausea, distension, flatus
Gastrointestinal.rationale: Absent bowel sounds after abdominal surgery may indicate postoperative ileus or developing obstruction. Correlate with nausea, abdominal distension, absence of flatus, and pain to differentiate expected ileus from complication. Notify the provider when bowel sounds remain absent with worsening distension or no flatus — obstruction can progress to ischemia.
Gastrointestinal.clinical_judgment_focus: Post-op abdominal assessment integrates bowel sounds with flatus, distension, and nausea.

Gastrointestinal.question: Which suffix means inflammation?
Gastrointestinal.rationale: The suffix '-itis' means inflammation of the attached structure — gastritis is stomach inflammation, meningitis is meningeal inflammation. Recognizing suffixes allows nurses to decode unfamiliar terms and anticipate clinical presentations. -osis means abnormal condition, -emia means blood condition, and -logy means study of.
Gastrointestinal.clinical_judgment_focus: Suffix recognition predicts condition type — inflammation, blood disorder, or disease state.

Renal.question: K+ 6.8 with peaked T waves — prepare first?
Renal.rationale: Severe hyperkalemia (K⁺ >6.5 with ECG changes): calcium gluconate IV first to stabilize myocardium — does not lower K⁺ but protects against arrhythmia while insulin/D50, kayexalate, or dialysis are prepared.
Renal.clinical_judgment_focus: Sequence hyperkalemia treatment: stabilize the myocardium first, then lower serum potassium.
Renal.clinical_why: Peaked T waves progress to widened QRS and sine wave arrest — continuous tele required.

Renal.question: ESRD on hemodialysis — which finding is urgent?
options: Absent thrill and bruit at fistula
Renal.rationale: Hemodialysis fistula requires thrill (palpable vibration) and bruit (audible whoosh) — absent both suggests thrombosis. Never BP or venipuncture on fistula arm.
Renal.clinical_judgment_focus: The dialysis fistula is the patient's lifeline — assess thrill and bruit every shift.
Renal.clinical_why: Clotted fistula loses dialysis access — surgical thrombectomy time-sensitive.

Renal.question: Post-op urine 15 mL/hr × 3 hr, BP 88/54, HR 118 — suspect?
Renal.rationale: Oliguria (<30 mL/hr) with hypotension and tachycardia post-op indicates hypovolemia — assess bleeding, IV access, fluid bolus per order, trend hemoglobin.
Renal.clinical_judgment_focus: Cluster oliguria with hemodynamic instability to identify hypovolemia early.
Renal.clinical_why: Undetected hemorrhage leads to shock and cardiac arrest — I&O and VS every 15 min early post-op.

Renal.question: Prerenal AKI BUN:Cr ratio typically:
Renal.rationale: Prerenal AKI results from decreased renal perfusion (dehydration, heart failure, sepsis) with intact kidney tissue. BUN rises disproportionately to creatinine, producing a BUN:Cr ratio >20:1. Intrinsic renal injury shows a ratio closer to 10:1; the distinction guides whether fluids will restore function.
Renal.clinical_judgment_focus: BUN:Cr ratio helps distinguish prerenal from intrinsic renal injury at the bedside.

Renal.question: Hematuria means:
Renal.rationale: Hematuria combines 'hemat/o' (blood) and '-uria' (urine condition) — blood present in the urine. Gross hematuria is visible; microscopic hematuria is detected on dipstick or microscopy. Both require provider notification to rule out infection, stones, trauma, or malignancy.
Renal.clinical_judgment_focus: Hematuria always warrants investigation — never normalize blood in urine.

Renal.question: The root 'nephr' refers to the:
Renal.rationale: The root 'nephr/o' refers to the kidney — seen in nephrology, nephritis, and nephrectomy. Understanding word roots builds vocabulary for renal assessment, medications, and patient education. Hepat- is liver, pulmon- is lung, and cardi- is heart.
Renal.clinical_judgment_focus: Word roots link terminology to body systems for faster clinical reasoning.

Endocrine.question: Type 1 diabetic BG 42 mg/dL, alert but confused — first action?
Endocrine.rationale: Conscious hypoglycemia (Rule of 15): 15 g fast carbohydrate (4 oz juice, glucose tabs), recheck in 15 min, repeat if still <70; glucagon or IV dextrose if unable to swallow.
Endocrine.clinical_judgment_focus: Apply the Rule of 15 for conscious hypoglycemia — treat fast, recheck, repeat as needed.
Endocrine.clinical_why: Never give insulin to hypoglycemic patient — seizure and death risk within minutes.

Endocrine.question: DKA: pH 7.18, K+ 3.2, glucose 480 — before insulin drip?
Endocrine.rationale: DKA: total body K⁺ depleted despite serum level — insulin drives K⁺ intracellularly. Replace K⁺ to ≥3.3 mEq/L before insulin infusion to prevent life-threatening hypokalemia.
Endocrine.clinical_judgment_focus: Anticipate insulin's effect on potassium before starting DKA protocol — prevent iatrogenic arrhythmia.
Endocrine.clinical_why: Insulin without K⁺ replacement can cause cardiac arrest from hypokalemia.

Endocrine.question: Highest lactic acidosis risk pair?
Endocrine.rationale: Metformin with IV iodinated contrast increases lactic acidosis risk — hold metformin 48 hr before and after contrast, verify renal function (eGFR).
Endocrine.clinical_judgment_focus: Know high-risk drug-contrast interactions — metformin plus IV contrast risks lactic acidosis.
Endocrine.clinical_why: Contrast-induced nephropathy plus metformin accumulation causes lethal lactic acidosis.

Endocrine.question: DKA vs HHNS: ketones are prominent in:
Endocrine.rationale: DKA features significant ketone production with metabolic acidosis (pH <7.3, anion gap elevated). HHS (hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state) presents with extreme hyperglycemia but minimal ketosis and less acidosis. Both are emergencies, but ketone prominence distinguishes DKA and guides insulin urgency.
Endocrine.clinical_judgment_focus: Ketone presence differentiates DKA from HHS — both need treatment but pathways differ.

Endocrine.question: Hyperglycemia indicates:
Endocrine.rationale: Hyperglycemia means blood glucose above normal limits — hyper- (excessive) + glyc (glucose) + -emia (blood condition). Sustained hyperglycemia can progress to DKA (type 1) or HHS (type 2) and requires insulin or protocol-driven treatment. Always verify glucose before administering insulin to prevent hypoglycemic events.
Endocrine.clinical_judgment_focus: Hyperglycemia terminology connects to actionable nursing assessment and insulin safety.

Musculoskeletal.question: Post total hip arthroplasty — which finding needs immediate action?
Musculoskeletal.rationale: Sudden SOB and tachycardia after orthopedic surgery suggests pulmonary embolism — immobilization and hypercoagulability. Assess SpO₂, prepare heparin/CT angiography per order.
Musculoskeletal.clinical_judgment_focus: Recognize post-op SOB as PE until ruled out — a silent killer after orthopedic surgery.
Musculoskeletal.clinical_why: PE is a leading cause of post-op death — don't attribute SOB to pain alone.

Musculoskeletal.question: Cast pain unrelieved by opioids with passive toe pain — suspect?
Musculoskeletal.rationale: Compartment syndrome: pain out of proportion, pain with passive stretch of toes, paresthesia, pallor, pulselessness (late). Elevate cast to heart level, bivalve cast, notify surgeon immediately.
Musculoskeletal.clinical_judgment_focus: Pain out of proportion to injury is the cardinal cue for compartment syndrome.
Musculoskeletal.clinical_why: Fasciotomy within 6 hours prevents permanent nerve and muscle necrosis.

Musculoskeletal.question: A positive Homans sign (calf pain on dorsiflexion) is:
options: Not reliable alone for DVT — use Wells criteria and ultrasound
Musculoskeletal.rationale: Homans sign (calf pain on dorsiflexion) is neither sensitive nor specific enough to diagnose DVT alone. Assess for unilateral calf swelling, warmth, erythema, and pain while applying Wells criteria and ordering ultrasound. Missed DVT is a leading cause of preventable pulmonary embolism in hospitalized patients.
Musculoskeletal.clinical_judgment_focus: Never rely on a single physical exam maneuver for DVT — use validated clinical decision tools.

Hematologic.question: Neutropenic patient temp 100.8°F — priority?
options: Blood cultures and notify for antibiotics
Hematologic.rationale: Febrile neutropenia (ANC <500 with temp ≥38.3°C): medical emergency — blood cultures ×2, broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour, avoid rectal temps and fresh flowers.
Hematologic.clinical_judgment_focus: Neutropenic fever is a 'treat first, diagnose second' emergency — do not wait for culture results to start antibiotics.
Hematologic.clinical_why: Neutropenic patients lack inflammatory response — fever may be only sign of sepsis.

Hematologic.question: Sickle cell: chest pain, fever, hypoxia — priority?
Hematologic.rationale: Acute chest syndrome in sickle cell: new pulmonary infiltrate, chest pain, fever, hypoxia — O₂, fluids, antibiotics, exchange transfusion may be needed. Mimics pneumonia.
Hematologic.clinical_judgment_focus: Acute chest syndrome in sickle cell is an emergency — oxygen, fluids, and provider notification.
Hematologic.clinical_why: Leading cause of death in sickle cell adults — early recognition reduces respiratory failure.

Hematologic.question: Transfusion: chills, fever, back pain — nurse should?
Hematologic.rationale: Acute hemolytic transfusion reaction: fever, chills, flank/back pain, hypotension during transfusion — stop immediately, keep IV open with NS, return blood to lab, notify provider.
Hematologic.clinical_judgment_focus: Any transfusion reaction with fever and back pain demands immediate cessation and investigation.
Hematologic.clinical_why: Continuing transfusion causes DIC, renal failure, and death — verify patient ID and blood unit at bedside.

Hematologic.question: HIT on heparin — priority action:
Hematologic.rationale: Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) is an immune-mediated paradox: platelets drop but thrombosis risk soars. All heparin products must be stopped immediately when HIT is suspected — continuing causes arterial and venous clots. Alternative anticoagulation (argatroban, bivalirudin) is needed; platelet transfusion is contraindicated unless bleeding.
Hematologic.clinical_judgment_focus: HIT is a clotting disorder disguised as low platelets — stop heparin immediately.

Prioritization.question: Which patient should the nurse see first?
options: Routine BG check due in 30 min
Prioritization.rationale: Prioritize unstable over stable: new dyspnea with SpO₂ 89% in CHF patient risks respiratory failure; post-op pain 5/10 and routine tasks can wait.
Prioritization.clinical_judgment_focus: Use ABC framework and instability to rank which patient needs the nurse first.
Prioritization.clinical_why: NCLEX and clinical practice use ABCs and acute vs chronic/unstable vs stable frameworks.

Prioritization.question: Four patients call the nurse simultaneously. Who should be assessed first?
options: Post-op day 1 requesting pain medication rated 6/10
Client with new onset chest pain and diaphoresis
Client awaiting discharge teaching for insulin injection
Client with a medication due in 30 minutes
Prioritization.rationale: New chest pain with diaphoresis suggests acute coronary syndrome — a potential life threat requiring immediate assessment (ABCs, ECG, vitals, provider notification). Pain medication, discharge teaching, and a medication due in 30 minutes are important but not emergent compared to possible myocardial infarction.
Prioritization.clinical_judgment_focus: Unstable vs. stable — acute cardiac symptoms outrank comfort and routine tasks.

Prioritization.question: After receiving report, which client should the nurse see first?
options: Diabetic client whose breakfast tray just arrived
Client with COPD whose SpO2 dropped from 94% to 88% with increased work of breathing
Client requesting assistance to the bathroom
Client scheduled for physical therapy at 1000
Prioritization.rationale: Declining oxygen saturation with increased work of breathing signals respiratory compromise — assess airway, position, O₂ delivery, and notify provider. Meal timing, toileting, and scheduled therapy are lower priority than potential respiratory failure.
Prioritization.clinical_judgment_focus: Trending vital sign changes trump scheduled activities.

Prioritization.question: The nurse has 15 minutes left in shift. Which task is best delegated to an unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP)?
options: Ambulating a post-op client day 1 after abdominal surgery
Feeding an alert stroke client with mild dysphagia
Obtaining routine vital signs on stable clients
Reinforcing discharge teaching about wound care
Prioritization.rationale: Routine vital signs on stable clients are within UAP scope when no unstable findings are expected. Post-op ambulation, dysphagia feeding, and discharge teaching require nursing assessment and judgment — inappropriate to delegate.
Prioritization.clinical_judgment_focus: Delegation requires matching task stability with caregiver scope.

Prioritization.question: Which finding requires immediate nursing intervention?
options: Report of dry mouth after starting antihistamine
Urine output 35 mL/hr for the past 2 hours
New onset confusion and slurred speech
Incisional pain rated 4/10 after analgesic given 1 hour ago
Prioritization.rationale: Acute confusion and slurred speech may indicate stroke, hypoglycemia, sepsis, or medication toxicity — all require immediate assessment (glucose, neuro checks, vitals). Dry mouth is an expected antihistamine effect; 35 mL/hr meets minimum adult output; mild pain after recent analgesic is monitored but not emergent.
Prioritization.clinical_judgment_focus: Neurological acute changes are always high priority.

Infection Control.question: C. difficile care — nurse should?
Infection Control.rationale: C. difficile spores resist alcohol-based hand rub — soap and water with friction removes spores. Contact precautions, dedicated equipment, bleach cleaning.
Infection Control.clinical_judgment_focus: Match hand hygiene method to the pathogen — spore-forming organisms require soap and water.
Infection Control.clinical_why: Alcohol gel gives false security — spores spread room-to-room causing outbreaks.

Infection Control.question: Which precaution type is required for pulmonary tuberculosis?
Infection Control.rationale: Pulmonary tuberculosis spreads via airborne droplet nuclei that remain suspended in room air for hours. Airborne precautions require a negative-pressure AIIR and N95 respirator (or PAPR) for all room entry. Droplet precautions are insufficient because TB particles are smaller than droplet transmission thresholds.
Infection Control.clinical_judgment_focus: Match isolation type to transmission — TB requires airborne, not droplet, precautions.

Infection Control.question: The nurse breaks which chain link by performing hand hygiene before patient care?
Infection Control.rationale: Hand hygiene before patient contact breaks the chain of infection at the mode of transmission link. Pathogens on healthcare worker hands are the most common vehicle for nosocomial spread between patients. The infectious agent, reservoir, and portal of entry are not directly interrupted by hand washing alone.
Infection Control.clinical_judgment_focus: Hand hygiene is the single most effective action to break nosocomial transmission.

Infection Control.question: Varicella (chickenpox) requires:
options: Both airborne and contact precautions until lesions crusted
Infection Control.rationale: Varicella is transmitted both by airborne droplet nuclei and direct contact with vesicle fluid. CDC requires airborne isolation plus contact precautions until all lesions are crusted over. Droplet or contact precautions alone do not provide adequate protection for this dual-mode pathogen.
Infection Control.clinical_judgment_focus: Some pathogens require combined precaution types — varicella needs airborne plus contact.

Infection Control.question: Vaccination primarily targets which chain link?
Infection Control.rationale: Vaccination targets the susceptible host link by stimulating adaptive immunity before pathogen exposure. Herd immunity reduces transmission by shrinking the pool of susceptible individuals in a population. Vaccines do not eliminate the agent or reservoir — they prepare the host defense.
Infection Control.clinical_judgment_focus: Vaccination is primary prevention targeting host susceptibility in the chain of infection.

Infection Control.question: Gram stain of Gram-negative organisms shows:
Infection Control.rationale: Gram-negative bacteria have a thin peptidoglycan layer and lipopolysaccharide outer membrane that does not retain crystal violet. They appear pink/red after the counterstain (safranin) in the Gram stain procedure. Purple cocci in clusters describe Gram-positive Staphylococcus; acid-fast staining identifies mycobacteria.
Infection Control.clinical_judgment_focus: Gram stain results guide empiric antibiotic selection while cultures are pending.

Leadership.question: Appropriate task to delegate to UAP?
options: Ambulate stable post-op with PT clearance
Leadership.rationale: Delegate stable, routine tasks with clear parameters to UAP — ambulation after PT clearance. Never delegate assessment, teaching, evaluation, or unstable patients.
Leadership.clinical_judgment_focus: Apply delegation rules: right task, right person, right circumstance, right direction, right supervision.
Leadership.clinical_why: RN retains accountability — inappropriate delegation is a leading cause of nursing malpractice.

Psychosocial Integrity.question: Therapeutic response to 'I want to die':
options: Are you having thoughts of killing yourself right now?
Psychosocial Integrity.rationale: Any statement suggesting suicidal intent requires direct, nonjudgmental assessment of active ideation and plan. Asking 'Are you having thoughts of killing yourself right now?' is the standard therapeutic and safety approach. Dismissing, delaying, or offering false reassurance fails to assess risk and may increase isolation.
Psychosocial Integrity.clinical_judgment_focus: Suicidal statements always warrant direct assessment — never avoid the question out of discomfort.

Psychosocial Integrity.question: Non-therapeutic communication is:
Psychosocial Integrity.rationale: Therapeutic communication validates the patient's experience without minimizing or correcting feelings. Giving false reassurance ('You'll feel better tomorrow') dismisses the patient's reality and shuts down dialogue. Reflection, open-ended questions, and offering self are therapeutic techniques that build trust.
Psychosocial Integrity.clinical_judgment_focus: Distinguish therapeutic from non-therapeutic responses — validation beats correction.

Psychosocial Integrity.question: CIWA-Ar score 22 indicates:
Psychosocial Integrity.rationale: A CIWA-Ar score ≥20 indicates severe alcohol withdrawal with risk of seizures and delirium tremens. Benzodiazepine protocol per facility guidelines is required urgently to prevent life-threatening complications. Scores 8–15 are moderate; below 8 may need monitoring only — but ≥20 demands immediate pharmacologic treatment.
Psychosocial Integrity.clinical_judgment_focus: CIWA-Ar scores guide benzodiazepine dosing urgency in alcohol withdrawal.

Psychosocial Integrity.question: Priority for command hallucinations to harm others:
Psychosocial Integrity.rationale: Command hallucinations directing harm to others require immediate safety assessment and 1:1 observation. The nurse must determine whether the patient can resist the commands and whether means and opportunity exist. Ignoring hallucinations or arguing about their reality does not address the imminent safety risk.
Psychosocial Integrity.clinical_judgment_focus: Command hallucinations with violence potential require immediate safety intervention.

Psychosocial Integrity.question: PHQ-9 item 9 positive requires:
Psychosocial Integrity.rationale: PHQ-9 item 9 specifically screens for active suicidal ideation in the past two weeks. A positive response mandates immediate suicide risk assessment — not routine follow-up or discharge. Suicidal ideation can exist even when mood appears improved; always assess directly.
Psychosocial Integrity.clinical_judgment_focus: PHQ-9 item 9 is a dedicated suicide screen — a positive answer triggers full risk assessment.

Psychosocial Integrity.question: Least restrictive intervention means:
options: Start with verbal de-escalation and milieu management
Psychosocial Integrity.rationale: The least restrictive environment principle requires starting with verbal de-escalation and milieu management. Restraint and seclusion are last resorts after less restrictive interventions have been attempted and documented. CMS regulations and nursing ethics mandate this hierarchy to protect patient dignity and safety.
Psychosocial Integrity.clinical_judgment_focus: Least restrictive intervention preserves dignity while maintaining safety.

Psychosocial Integrity.question: Trauma-informed care emphasizes:
Psychosocial Integrity.rationale: Trauma-informed care prioritizes safety, trustworthiness, choice, collaboration, and empowerment. Forcing confrontation of trauma details or using punitive approaches can retraumatize the patient. Building a predictable, respectful environment supports recovery and therapeutic engagement.
Psychosocial Integrity.clinical_judgment_focus: Trauma-informed principles reshape how nurses approach behavior and build therapeutic alliances.

Psychosocial Integrity.question: Restraint/seclusion requires:
options: Provider order, least restrictive alternatives attempted, continuous monitoring
Psychosocial Integrity.rationale: Restraint and seclusion require a provider order, documented least-restrictive alternatives, and continuous face-to-face monitoring. Regulatory standards (CMS, Joint Commission) mandate time-limited orders and debriefing after release. Nurses cannot apply restraints indefinitely without reassessment and a valid order.
Psychosocial Integrity.clinical_judgment_focus: Restraint use is regulated — documentation and monitoring are as important as the intervention itself.

Psychosocial Integrity.question: A patient states 'Nobody cares about me.' Which response uses therapeutic communication?
options: That's not true — your family visits.
You should try to be more positive.
Psychosocial Integrity.rationale: Reflection ('You feel alone right now') validates emotion without arguing or minimizing — builds trust for further suicide and depression assessment.
Psychosocial Integrity.clinical_judgment_focus: Therapeutic communication prioritizes patient feelings over correction.
Psychosocial Integrity.clinical_why: Correcting or dismissing feelings shuts down disclosure of suicidal ideation.

Psychosocial Integrity.question: A client states, 'I have a plan to overdose tonight with my pills at home.' What is the nurse's priority response?
options: Stay with client, remove means, initiate suicide precautions per protocol, notify provider
Schedule follow-up outpatient appointment next week
Ask client to sign a no-harm contract and document
Encourage client to think about family impact
Psychosocial Integrity.rationale: Active suicidal ideation with plan, means, and timeline is a psychiatric emergency. Constant observation, environmental safety (remove pills/sharps), provider notification, and possible inpatient care take priority. No-harm contracts are not substitutes for safety precautions.
Psychosocial Integrity.clinical_judgment_focus: Suicide risk with plan and means requires immediate safety intervention.

Pharmacological Therapies.question: Serotonin syndrome includes:
Pharmacological Therapies.rationale: Serotonin syndrome results from excess serotonergic activity, often from combining SSRIs, SNRIs, MAOIs, or linezolid. Hyperreflexia, clonus (especially inducible ankle clonus), agitation, and autonomic instability are hallmark signs. This is a medical emergency — stop offending agents and notify the provider for cyproheptadine or supportive care.
Pharmacological Therapies.clinical_judgment_focus: Recognize serotonin syndrome early — clonus and hyperreflexia distinguish it from other causes of agitation.

Pharmacological Therapies.question: Lithium teaching includes:
options: Maintain consistent sodium and fluid intake
Pharmacological Therapies.rationale: Lithium has a narrow therapeutic index and is excreted renally — dehydration and sodium loss increase toxicity. Patients must maintain consistent fluid intake and sodium consumption; illness with vomiting/diarrhea needs urgent evaluation. Therapeutic drug levels require regular monitoring; missed doses should not be doubled.
Pharmacological Therapies.clinical_judgment_focus: Lithium toxicity is preventable with consistent sodium, fluid intake, and level monitoring.

Pharmacological Therapies.question: Antipsychotic monitoring includes:
options: Weight, glucose, lipids for metabolic syndrome
Pharmacological Therapies.rationale: Second-generation antipsychotics carry significant metabolic risks including weight gain, hyperglycemia, and dyslipidemia. Baseline and ongoing monitoring of weight, BMI, fasting glucose, and lipids is standard of care. Failure to monitor can result in new-onset diabetes and cardiovascular disease in psychiatric patients.
Pharmacological Therapies.clinical_judgment_focus: Metabolic monitoring is essential with antipsychotics — psychiatric medications affect physical health.

Pharmacological Therapies.question: Antimicrobial stewardship prioritizes:
options: Prophylactic antibiotics for all surgical patients indefinitely
Pharmacological Therapies.rationale: Antimicrobial stewardship promotes the shortest effective antibiotic course guided by culture results and clinical guidelines. Broad-spectrum empiric therapy should be de-escalated once the pathogen is identified to reduce resistance and C. diff risk. Saving leftover antibiotics and indefinite surgical prophylaxis promote resistance and adverse outcomes.
Pharmacological Therapies.clinical_judgment_focus: Stewardship protects future patients by limiting resistance and C. difficile.

Pharmacological Therapies.question: An antibiotic is best defined as:
options: A drug that inhibits or destroys bacteria
Pharmacological Therapies.rationale: Antibiotics are agents that inhibit or destroy bacteria — anti- (against) + bio (life) + -tic (pertaining to). They are ineffective against viruses; inappropriate use drives antimicrobial resistance. Culture and sensitivity guides selection of the narrowest effective spectrum.
Pharmacological Therapies.clinical_judgment_focus: Antibiotic knowledge prevents misuse and supports stewardship conversations.

Pharmacological Therapies.question: Order: Digoxin 0.125 mg PO daily. Apical pulse 52/min. Nurse should?
Pharmacological Therapies.rationale: Digoxin has narrow therapeutic index — hold if apical pulse <60 bpm (adult) or per facility policy, assess for toxicity (nausea, vision changes, arrhythmias), notify provider.
Pharmacological Therapies.clinical_judgment_focus: Medication safety requires assessment before administration.
Pharmacological Therapies.clinical_why: Bradycardia may worsen to heart block — hypokalemia increases digoxin toxicity risk.

Pharmacological Therapies.question: Before administering insulin from a vial, the nurse's priority safety action is:
Pharmacological Therapies.rationale: Insulin is an ISMP high-alert medication requiring an independent double-check before administration. Only regular insulin may be given IV; other formulations are for subcutaneous use only. Wrong insulin type or dose is a leading cause of preventable hypoglycemic injury and death.
Pharmacological Therapies.clinical_judgment_focus: High-alert medications demand independent double-checks and route verification.

Pharmacological Therapies.question: Post-op patient received IV morphine 4 mg. RR 10/min, difficult to arouse. Nurse should:
options: Stop opioid, stimulate patient, apply O2, prepare naloxone
Pharmacological Therapies.rationale: Opioid-induced respiratory depression is signaled by RR <12/min, sedation, and pinpoint pupils. Hold the opioid, stimulate the patient, apply oxygen, and prepare naloxone per protocol. Never leave a sedated patient unattended after opioid administration — continuous respiratory assessment is required.
Pharmacological Therapies.clinical_judgment_focus: Opioid safety requires respiratory assessment before and after every dose.

Maternal-Newborn.question: Late decelerations during labor — first action?
Maternal-Newborn.rationale: Late decelerations indicate uteroplacental insufficiency — the fetus is not receiving adequate oxygen during contractions. Stop oxytocin if infusing, perform intrauterine resuscitation (position change, O2, IV fluids), and notify the provider. Increasing oxytocin or ignoring the monitor worsens fetal hypoxia and risks fetal demise.
Maternal-Newborn.clinical_judgment_focus: Late decels = fetal hypoxia — intrauterine resuscitation is the nurse's immediate role.

Maternal-Newborn.question: Postpartum fundus boggy — intervene with:
Maternal-Newborn.rationale: A boggy, deviated uterus after delivery indicates uterine atony — the leading cause of postpartum hemorrhage. Fundal massage is first-line to stimulate contraction and reduce bleeding. Ice packs and bed rest do not address atony; prompt massage can prevent hemorrhagic shock.
Maternal-Newborn.clinical_judgment_focus: Boggy fundus = atony = hemorrhage risk — massage before medications when possible.

Maternal-Newborn.question: Preeclampsia severe feature includes:
Maternal-Newborn.rationale: RUQ/epigastric pain in preeclampsia suggests liver capsule distension and possible HELLP syndrome. This is a severe feature requiring immediate provider notification and likely magnesium sulfate and delivery planning. Mild ankle edema and Braxton Hicks contractions are common and not severe features.
Maternal-Newborn.clinical_judgment_focus: RUQ pain in preeclampsia signals severe disease — do not dismiss as heartburn.

Maternal-Newborn.question: Shoulder dystocia maneuver:
Maternal-Newborn.rationale: Shoulder dystocia requires McRoberts maneuver (hyperflex thighs) plus suprapubic pressure to free the anterior shoulder. Fundal pressure pushes the baby further into impaction and is contraindicated. This is an obstetric emergency — call for help and document time of maneuvers.
Maternal-Newborn.clinical_judgment_focus: Shoulder dystocia has a defined maneuver sequence — fundal pressure makes it worse.

Maternal-Newborn.question: Newborn APGAR 4 at 1 minute — nurse:
options: Continue resuscitation per NRP and reassess at 5 min
Maternal-Newborn.rationale: An APGAR of 4 at 1 minute indicates the newborn needs active resuscitation per Neonatal Resuscitation Program (NRP) algorithm. Continue ventilation, warming, and stimulation; reassess at 5 minutes. Never stop intervention or discharge a compromised newborn without full resuscitation efforts.
Maternal-Newborn.clinical_judgment_focus: Low APGAR triggers NRP algorithm — reassess at 5 minutes, never abandon resuscitation early.

Maternal-Newborn.question: Magnesium toxicity sign:
Maternal-Newborn.rationale: Absent deep tendon reflexes are the earliest sign of magnesium sulfate toxicity. Also monitor respiratory rate (>12/min) and urine output (>25–30 mL/hr) before each dose. Calcium gluconate is the antidote for severe toxicity; report absent reflexes immediately.
Maternal-Newborn.clinical_judgment_focus: Magnesium toxicity presents with lost reflexes before respiratory arrest — monitor before each dose.

Maternal-Newborn.question: Cord prolapse nursing action:
options: Knee-chest, elevate presenting part, notify for emergency delivery
Push cord back into vagina repeatedly
Maternal-Newborn.rationale: Cord prolapse compresses the umbilical cord and cuts off fetal oxygen supply — an obstetric emergency. Knee-chest or Trendelenburg position relieves pressure while the nurse elevates the presenting part manually. Never attempt to push the cord back into the vagina; prepare for emergency cesarean delivery.
Maternal-Newborn.clinical_judgment_focus: Cord prolapse is a time-critical obstetric emergency — relieve compression while awaiting delivery.

Maternal-Newborn.question: Postpartum psychosis requires:
options: Immediate psychiatric care and infant safety measures
Maternal-Newborn.rationale: Postpartum psychosis is a psychiatric emergency occurring within days to 4 weeks after delivery. It requires immediate psychiatric care, possible hospitalization, and infant safety measures including supervised contact. Outpatient follow-up alone is insufficient — the mother may have delusions commanding harm to self or infant.
Maternal-Newborn.clinical_judgment_focus: Postpartum psychosis requires emergency psychiatric intervention and infant protection.

Maternal-Newborn.question: A pregnant patient at 32 weeks reports sudden severe headache and visual changes. Priority action?
options: Assess BP and notify provider immediately
Maternal-Newborn.rationale: Sudden headache and visual changes at 32 weeks suggest severe preeclampsia — assess BP, proteinuria, reflexes; magnesium and delivery planning may be needed.
Maternal-Newborn.clinical_judgment_focus: Maternal and fetal safety — preeclampsia is life-threatening.
Maternal-Newborn.clinical_why: Eclampsia risk rises with severe features — maternal seizure and placental abruption threaten both lives.

Pediatric Nursing.question: 14-day-old with 38.3°C fever — priority:
options: Full sepsis evaluation per neonatal protocol
Pediatric Nursing.rationale: Fever ≥38°C (100.4°F) in an infant under 28–60 days is a medical emergency due to immature immune function. Full sepsis workup (blood culture, UA, LP per protocol) and empiric antibiotics are required — not outpatient management. Young infants can deteriorate rapidly from occult bacteremia or meningitis.
Pediatric Nursing.clinical_judgment_focus: Neonatal fever is always serious — young infants need sepsis evaluation, not outpatient care.

Pediatric Nursing.question: Physiologic jaundice typically appears:
Pediatric Nursing.rationale: Physiologic jaundice in term newborns typically appears on days 2–3 as fetal RBC breakdown increases bilirubin load. Jaundice within the first 24 hours is pathologic and requires immediate evaluation for hemolysis or infection. Breastfed infants can have prolonged physiologic jaundice but still follow the typical onset pattern.
Pediatric Nursing.clinical_judgment_focus: Jaundice timing distinguishes physiologic from pathologic hyperbilirubinemia.

Pediatric Nursing.question: A 4-month-old should achieve which developmental milestone?
Pediatric Nursing.rationale: At 4 months, infants roll back to side, hold head steady, reach for objects, and social smile — walking (12 mo) and two-word phrases (18–24 mo) are much later.
Pediatric Nursing.clinical_judgment_focus: Age-appropriate expectations guide assessment and parent teaching.
Pediatric Nursing.clinical_why: Developmental delay screening at well-child visits triggers early intervention for motor and cognitive deficits.

Pediatric Nursing.question: A 2-year-old is admitted with suspected ingestion. Which assessment finding is most urgent?
options: Tearful when parent leaves the room
Drooling and inability to swallow with stridor
Pediatric Nursing.rationale: Drooling, inability to swallow, and stridor suggest airway edema or obstruction from caustic or foreign body ingestion — immediate airway assessment and escalation. Separation anxiety is expected; growth and immunization history are not emergent.
Pediatric Nursing.clinical_judgment_focus: Pediatric airway compromise is always first priority.

Safety & Infection Control.question: First action when entering room of patient on contact precautions?
options: Perform hand hygiene and don gown/gloves
Safety & Infection Control.rationale: Contact precautions require hand hygiene before donning gown and gloves on room entry. PPE is donned before contact and doffed inside the room to prevent environmental contamination. An N95 is not required for contact precautions — it is reserved for airborne pathogens like TB.
Safety & Infection Control.clinical_judgment_focus: Correct PPE sequence prevents self-contamination during donning and doffing.

Safety & Infection Control.question: CLABSI prevention includes:
options: Central line bundle: hand hygiene, maximal barrier, chlorhexidine prep, review necessity
Prophylactic IV antibiotics for all lines
Safety & Infection Control.rationale: The central line bundle combines hand hygiene, maximal sterile barrier, chlorhexidine skin prep, optimal site selection, and daily necessity review. This evidence-based bundle significantly reduces CLABSI rates in ICU and acute care settings. Routine prophylactic antibiotics and daily dressing changes without indication are not bundle components.
Safety & Infection Control.clinical_judgment_focus: Evidence-based bundles standardize best practices and reduce preventable infections.

Safety & Infection Control.question: A nurse with influenza should:
options: Stay home until fever-free per policy; antivirals if indicated
Wear N95 and continue all assignments
Safety & Infection Control.rationale: Healthcare workers with influenza must stay home per occupational health policy to prevent nosocomial transmission. Influenza is contagious before symptoms peak and vulnerable patients (elderly, immunocompromised) face high mortality. Working with a surgical mask still exposes patients; antivirals are treatment, not a substitute for exclusion.
Safety & Infection Control.clinical_judgment_focus: Ill healthcare workers are a patient safety hazard — occupational health policies protect vulnerable patients.

Safety & Infection Control.question: Fire response — nurse's first action when smoke is seen in a patient room?
Safety & Infection Control.rationale: RACE protocol: Rescue patients in immediate danger first, then pull Alarm, Contain fire/smoke by closing doors, Extinguish if trained and fire is small, Evacuate vertically per facility plan.
Safety & Infection Control.clinical_judgment_focus: Life safety protocols prioritize human rescue.
Safety & Infection Control.clinical_why: Horizontal evacuation may be impossible — rescue prevents smoke inhalation deaths before fire spreads.

Safety & Infection Control.question: Before administering a scheduled opioid, the nurse notes respirations 8/min and the client is difficult to arouse. What is the priority action?
options: Administer the opioid as scheduled and recheck in 30 minutes
Hold the opioid, assess further, and notify provider — consider naloxone per protocol
Offer a snack to stimulate the client
Safety & Infection Control.rationale: Respiratory depression (RR <10) with decreased level of consciousness indicates opioid toxicity — hold further opioids, maintain airway, stimulate/support respirations, notify provider, and prepare naloxone per facility protocol. Administering another dose could cause arrest.
Safety & Infection Control.clinical_judgment_focus: Medication safety — assess before administering sedating drugs.

Safety & Infection Control.question: A nurse discovers a client on the floor beside the bed, alert and complaining of hip pain. What is the first action?
options: Help client back to bed immediately
Assess client and call for help per fall protocol
Obtain x-ray order before moving client
Safety & Infection Control.rationale: After a fall, assess for injury and neurological status before moving the client. Call for help and follow facility fall protocol — do not lift alone if hip fracture suspected. Incident reporting follows assessment and stabilization, not before.
Safety & Infection Control.clinical_judgment_focus: Client safety — assess injury before movement or documentation tasks.

Safety & Infection Control.question: Which action prevents central line-associated bloodstream infection (CLABSI)?
options: Change transparent dressing every 7 days per policy and use chlorhexidine scrub
Flush with heparin only when line is used
Remove cap and leave hub open during transport
Safety & Infection Control.rationale: CLABSI bundles include hand hygiene, maximal barrier precautions on insertion, chlorhexidine skin prep, appropriate dressing changes, and hub disinfection before access. Open hubs and routine prophylactic antibiotics are not evidence-based and increase risk.
Safety & Infection Control.clinical_judgment_focus: Infection prevention bundles are high-yield NCLEX Safety items.

Safety & Infection Control.question: A nurse is preparing to hang IV potassium chloride 10 mEq in 100 mL over 1 hour. Which verification step is essential?
options: Confirm concentration and maximum infusion rate per policy; use pump
Administer as IV push if client is symptomatic
Dilute in D5W only without checking current fluids
Double the rate if K+ is critically low
Safety & Infection Control.rationale: IV potassium is never given IV push — it causes cardiac arrest. Concentration, dilution, and infusion rate must match policy (typically ≤10 mEq/hr peripheral, higher central with monitoring). Always verify against current fluids and renal status.
Safety & Infection Control.clinical_judgment_focus: High-alert medication safety — KCl errors are lethal.

all_questions.question: Left-sided heart failure — which finding is most consistent?
all_questions.rationale: Left-sided HF reduces LV output, causing blood to back up into pulmonary circulation — crackles, orthopnea, pink frothy sputum, and S₃ gallop. JVD and peripheral edema suggest right-sided failure.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Differentiate left- versus right-sided failure by identifying where congestion backs up — lungs vs. systemic circulation.
all_questions.clinical_why: Differentiating left vs right HF guides diuretic use, positioning (high Fowler's), and monitoring (daily weights, I&O).

all_questions.question: Appropriate SpO2 target for COPD on supplemental O2?
all_questions.rationale: COPD patients are chronic CO₂ retainers; hypoxic drive maintains ventilation. Target SpO₂ 88–92% to prevent CO₂ narcosis while treating exacerbation.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Balance oxygenation with ventilation risk — chronic retainers need lower SpO2 targets than acute patients.
all_questions.clinical_why: Titrate O₂ to lowest saturation meeting target — ABG confirms pH and PaCO₂ trends.

all_questions.question: Suspected stroke within 45 minutes — first priority?
all_questions.rationale: Stroke protocol: establish last known well, ABCs, IV access, blood glucose (hypoglycemia mimics stroke), CT head, then consider tPA if ischemic and within window.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Time-critical stroke care still requires glucose verification and imaging before thrombolytics.
all_questions.clinical_why: Giving tPA before ruling out hemorrhage or hypoglycemia causes catastrophic harm.

all_questions.question: Cirrhosis with confusion and asterixis — priority intervention?
all_questions.rationale: Hepatic encephalopathy from ammonia accumulation — lactulose acidifies colon, traps ammonia, increases bowel movements. Protein restriction is temporary, not permanent.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Connect neuro changes in liver disease to ammonia accumulation and initiate targeted treatment.
all_questions.clinical_why: Asterixis and confusion can progress to coma — monitor mental status and hold sedatives that worsen encephalopathy.

all_questions.question: K+ 6.8 with peaked T waves — prepare first?
all_questions.rationale: Severe hyperkalemia (K⁺ >6.5 with ECG changes): calcium gluconate IV first to stabilize myocardium — does not lower K⁺ but protects against arrhythmia while insulin/D50, kayexalate, or dialysis are prepared.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Sequence hyperkalemia treatment: stabilize the myocardium first, then lower serum potassium.
all_questions.clinical_why: Peaked T waves progress to widened QRS and sine wave arrest — continuous tele required.

all_questions.question: Type 1 diabetic BG 42 mg/dL, alert but confused — first action?
all_questions.rationale: Conscious hypoglycemia (Rule of 15): 15 g fast carbohydrate (4 oz juice, glucose tabs), recheck in 15 min, repeat if still <70; glucagon or IV dextrose if unable to swallow.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Apply the Rule of 15 for conscious hypoglycemia — treat fast, recheck, repeat as needed.
all_questions.clinical_why: Never give insulin to hypoglycemic patient — seizure and death risk within minutes.

all_questions.question: Post total hip arthroplasty — which finding needs immediate action?
all_questions.rationale: Sudden SOB and tachycardia after orthopedic surgery suggests pulmonary embolism — immobilization and hypercoagulability. Assess SpO₂, prepare heparin/CT angiography per order.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Recognize post-op SOB as PE until ruled out — a silent killer after orthopedic surgery.
all_questions.clinical_why: PE is a leading cause of post-op death — don't attribute SOB to pain alone.

all_questions.question: Neutropenic patient temp 100.8°F — priority?
options: Blood cultures and notify for antibiotics
all_questions.rationale: Febrile neutropenia (ANC <500 with temp ≥38.3°C): medical emergency — blood cultures ×2, broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour, avoid rectal temps and fresh flowers.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Neutropenic fever is a 'treat first, diagnose second' emergency — do not wait for culture results to start antibiotics.
all_questions.clinical_why: Neutropenic patients lack inflammatory response — fever may be only sign of sepsis.

all_questions.question: Which patient should the nurse see first?
options: Routine BG check due in 30 min
all_questions.rationale: Prioritize unstable over stable: new dyspnea with SpO₂ 89% in CHF patient risks respiratory failure; post-op pain 5/10 and routine tasks can wait.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Use ABC framework and instability to rank which patient needs the nurse first.
all_questions.clinical_why: NCLEX and clinical practice use ABCs and acute vs chronic/unstable vs stable frameworks.

all_questions.question: DKA: pH 7.18, K+ 3.2, glucose 480 — before insulin drip?
all_questions.rationale: DKA: total body K⁺ depleted despite serum level — insulin drives K⁺ intracellularly. Replace K⁺ to ≥3.3 mEq/L before insulin infusion to prevent life-threatening hypokalemia.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Anticipate insulin's effect on potassium before starting DKA protocol — prevent iatrogenic arrhythmia.
all_questions.clinical_why: Insulin without K⁺ replacement can cause cardiac arrest from hypokalemia.

all_questions.question: Cast pain unrelieved by opioids with passive toe pain — suspect?
all_questions.rationale: Compartment syndrome: pain out of proportion, pain with passive stretch of toes, paresthesia, pallor, pulselessness (late). Elevate cast to heart level, bivalve cast, notify surgeon immediately.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Pain out of proportion to injury is the cardinal cue for compartment syndrome.
all_questions.clinical_why: Fasciotomy within 6 hours prevents permanent nerve and muscle necrosis.

all_questions.question: Most important warfarin teaching?
all_questions.rationale: Warfarin inhibits vitamin K-dependent clotting factors — consistent dietary vitamin K prevents INR swings; teach bleeding precautions and avoid NSAIDs without provider approval.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Patient education on warfarin must address dietary consistency, not just medication timing.
all_questions.clinical_why: Supratherapeutic INR causes GI and intracranial bleeding — subtherapeutic INR fails anticoagulation.

all_questions.question: Asthma with silent chest and exhaustion indicates?
all_questions.rationale: Silent chest in asthma means minimal air movement despite obstruction — exhaustion and impending respiratory failure, not improvement. Prepare epinephrine, assisted ventilation.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Silent chest in asthma is a danger sign — do not interpret absence of wheeze as improvement.
all_questions.clinical_why: Wheezing may decrease as patient fatigues — worsening obstruction, not resolution.

all_questions.question: SCI T4: pounding headache, BP 210/118 — first action?
all_questions.rationale: Autonomic dysreflexia (T6 and above): pounding headache, hypertension, bradycardia — sit upright, loosen constrictive clothing, identify trigger (full bladder, fecal impaction), notify provider.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Autonomic dysreflexia in SCI above T6 requires immediate upright positioning and trigger removal.
all_questions.clinical_why: BP can exceed 300 mmHg causing stroke — never place flat (increases cerebral perfusion pressure).

all_questions.question: Acute pancreatitis initial management?
all_questions.rationale: Acute pancreatitis: NPO to rest pancreas, aggressive IV fluids (third-spacing), pain control, monitor for ARDS and hypocalcemia. Oral intake resumes when lipase trends down and pain resolves.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Bowel rest and hydration are foundational in acute pancreatitis before nutrition is reintroduced.
all_questions.clinical_why: Early feeding in severe pancreatitis can worsen inflammation — follow enteral protocol when stable.

all_questions.question: ESRD on hemodialysis — which finding is urgent?
options: Absent thrill and bruit at fistula
all_questions.rationale: Hemodialysis fistula requires thrill (palpable vibration) and bruit (audible whoosh) — absent both suggests thrombosis. Never BP or venipuncture on fistula arm.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: The dialysis fistula is the patient's lifeline — assess thrill and bruit every shift.
all_questions.clinical_why: Clotted fistula loses dialysis access — surgical thrombectomy time-sensitive.

all_questions.question: C. difficile care — nurse should?
all_questions.rationale: C. difficile spores resist alcohol-based hand rub — soap and water with friction removes spores. Contact precautions, dedicated equipment, bleach cleaning.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Match hand hygiene method to the pathogen — spore-forming organisms require soap and water.
all_questions.clinical_why: Alcohol gel gives false security — spores spread room-to-room causing outbreaks.

all_questions.question: Sickle cell: chest pain, fever, hypoxia — priority?
all_questions.rationale: Acute chest syndrome in sickle cell: new pulmonary infiltrate, chest pain, fever, hypoxia — O₂, fluids, antibiotics, exchange transfusion may be needed. Mimics pneumonia.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Acute chest syndrome in sickle cell is an emergency — oxygen, fluids, and provider notification.
all_questions.clinical_why: Leading cause of death in sickle cell adults — early recognition reduces respiratory failure.

all_questions.question: Post-op urine 15 mL/hr × 3 hr, BP 88/54, HR 118 — suspect?
all_questions.rationale: Oliguria (<30 mL/hr) with hypotension and tachycardia post-op indicates hypovolemia — assess bleeding, IV access, fluid bolus per order, trend hemoglobin.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Cluster oliguria with hemodynamic instability to identify hypovolemia early.
all_questions.clinical_why: Undetected hemorrhage leads to shock and cardiac arrest — I&O and VS every 15 min early post-op.

all_questions.question: Highest lactic acidosis risk pair?
all_questions.rationale: Metformin with IV iodinated contrast increases lactic acidosis risk — hold metformin 48 hr before and after contrast, verify renal function (eGFR).
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Know high-risk drug-contrast interactions — metformin plus IV contrast risks lactic acidosis.
all_questions.clinical_why: Contrast-induced nephropathy plus metformin accumulation causes lethal lactic acidosis.

all_questions.question: Transfusion: chills, fever, back pain — nurse should?
all_questions.rationale: Acute hemolytic transfusion reaction: fever, chills, flank/back pain, hypotension during transfusion — stop immediately, keep IV open with NS, return blood to lab, notify provider.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Any transfusion reaction with fever and back pain demands immediate cessation and investigation.
all_questions.clinical_why: Continuing transfusion causes DIC, renal failure, and death — verify patient ID and blood unit at bedside.

all_questions.question: Appropriate task to delegate to UAP?
options: Ambulate stable post-op with PT clearance
all_questions.rationale: Delegate stable, routine tasks with clear parameters to UAP — ambulation after PT clearance. Never delegate assessment, teaching, evaluation, or unstable patients.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Apply delegation rules: right task, right person, right circumstance, right direction, right supervision.
all_questions.clinical_why: RN retains accountability — inappropriate delegation is a leading cause of nursing malpractice.

all_questions.question: Therapeutic response to 'I want to die':
options: Are you having thoughts of killing yourself right now?
all_questions.rationale: Any statement suggesting suicidal intent requires direct, nonjudgmental assessment of active ideation and plan. Asking 'Are you having thoughts of killing yourself right now?' is the standard therapeutic and safety approach. Dismissing, delaying, or offering false reassurance fails to assess risk and may increase isolation.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Suicidal statements always warrant direct assessment — never avoid the question out of discomfort.

all_questions.question: Non-therapeutic communication is:
all_questions.rationale: Therapeutic communication validates the patient's experience without minimizing or correcting feelings. Giving false reassurance ('You'll feel better tomorrow') dismisses the patient's reality and shuts down dialogue. Reflection, open-ended questions, and offering self are therapeutic techniques that build trust.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Distinguish therapeutic from non-therapeutic responses — validation beats correction.

all_questions.question: CIWA-Ar score 22 indicates:
all_questions.rationale: A CIWA-Ar score ≥20 indicates severe alcohol withdrawal with risk of seizures and delirium tremens. Benzodiazepine protocol per facility guidelines is required urgently to prevent life-threatening complications. Scores 8–15 are moderate; below 8 may need monitoring only — but ≥20 demands immediate pharmacologic treatment.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: CIWA-Ar scores guide benzodiazepine dosing urgency in alcohol withdrawal.

all_questions.question: Priority for command hallucinations to harm others:
all_questions.rationale: Command hallucinations directing harm to others require immediate safety assessment and 1:1 observation. The nurse must determine whether the patient can resist the commands and whether means and opportunity exist. Ignoring hallucinations or arguing about their reality does not address the imminent safety risk.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Command hallucinations with violence potential require immediate safety intervention.

all_questions.question: PHQ-9 item 9 positive requires:
all_questions.rationale: PHQ-9 item 9 specifically screens for active suicidal ideation in the past two weeks. A positive response mandates immediate suicide risk assessment — not routine follow-up or discharge. Suicidal ideation can exist even when mood appears improved; always assess directly.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: PHQ-9 item 9 is a dedicated suicide screen — a positive answer triggers full risk assessment.

all_questions.question: Serotonin syndrome includes:
all_questions.rationale: Serotonin syndrome results from excess serotonergic activity, often from combining SSRIs, SNRIs, MAOIs, or linezolid. Hyperreflexia, clonus (especially inducible ankle clonus), agitation, and autonomic instability are hallmark signs. This is a medical emergency — stop offending agents and notify the provider for cyproheptadine or supportive care.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Recognize serotonin syndrome early — clonus and hyperreflexia distinguish it from other causes of agitation.

all_questions.question: Least restrictive intervention means:
options: Start with verbal de-escalation and milieu management
all_questions.rationale: The least restrictive environment principle requires starting with verbal de-escalation and milieu management. Restraint and seclusion are last resorts after less restrictive interventions have been attempted and documented. CMS regulations and nursing ethics mandate this hierarchy to protect patient dignity and safety.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Least restrictive intervention preserves dignity while maintaining safety.

all_questions.question: Lithium teaching includes:
options: Maintain consistent sodium and fluid intake
all_questions.rationale: Lithium has a narrow therapeutic index and is excreted renally — dehydration and sodium loss increase toxicity. Patients must maintain consistent fluid intake and sodium consumption; illness with vomiting/diarrhea needs urgent evaluation. Therapeutic drug levels require regular monitoring; missed doses should not be doubled.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Lithium toxicity is preventable with consistent sodium, fluid intake, and level monitoring.

all_questions.question: Trauma-informed care emphasizes:
all_questions.rationale: Trauma-informed care prioritizes safety, trustworthiness, choice, collaboration, and empowerment. Forcing confrontation of trauma details or using punitive approaches can retraumatize the patient. Building a predictable, respectful environment supports recovery and therapeutic engagement.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Trauma-informed principles reshape how nurses approach behavior and build therapeutic alliances.

all_questions.question: Antipsychotic monitoring includes:
options: Weight, glucose, lipids for metabolic syndrome
all_questions.rationale: Second-generation antipsychotics carry significant metabolic risks including weight gain, hyperglycemia, and dyslipidemia. Baseline and ongoing monitoring of weight, BMI, fasting glucose, and lipids is standard of care. Failure to monitor can result in new-onset diabetes and cardiovascular disease in psychiatric patients.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Metabolic monitoring is essential with antipsychotics — psychiatric medications affect physical health.

all_questions.question: Restraint/seclusion requires:
options: Provider order, least restrictive alternatives attempted, continuous monitoring
all_questions.rationale: Restraint and seclusion require a provider order, documented least-restrictive alternatives, and continuous face-to-face monitoring. Regulatory standards (CMS, Joint Commission) mandate time-limited orders and debriefing after release. Nurses cannot apply restraints indefinitely without reassessment and a valid order.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Restraint use is regulated — documentation and monitoring are as important as the intervention itself.

all_questions.question: Which shock type presents with warm flushed skin early then hypotension?
all_questions.rationale: Septic (distributive) shock causes massive vasodilation, producing warm, flushed skin in early phases. As shock progresses, compensatory mechanisms fail and hypotension develops despite warm extremities initially. Cardiogenic shock presents with cool, clammy skin; hypovolemic shock shows pale, diaphoretic extremities.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Early septic shock may look 'warm' — do not be reassured by flushed skin in a febrile patient.

all_questions.question: COPD chronic baseline may include:
all_questions.rationale: Chronic COPD patients retain CO2 and compensate with renal bicarbonate retention, producing compensated respiratory acidosis. Their baseline PaCO2 is elevated with near-normal pH — this is their compensated 'normal.' Over-oxygenating these patients can shift this fragile balance and cause acute CO2 narcosis.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Know the compensated baseline in COPD — their 'abnormal' ABG may be their normal.

all_questions.question: Refractory hypotension in septic shock after fluids — next:
all_questions.rationale: When septic shock remains hypotensive after adequate fluid resuscitation, vasopressor support is indicated. Norepinephrine is often first-line to achieve MAP ≥65 mmHg and restore organ perfusion. Additional fluid boluses alone without vasopressors in refractory shock delay critical perfusion support.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Escalate from fluids to vasopressors when septic shock does not respond to volume resuscitation.

all_questions.question: Prerenal AKI BUN:Cr ratio typically:
all_questions.rationale: Prerenal AKI results from decreased renal perfusion (dehydration, heart failure, sepsis) with intact kidney tissue. BUN rises disproportionately to creatinine, producing a BUN:Cr ratio >20:1. Intrinsic renal injury shows a ratio closer to 10:1; the distinction guides whether fluids will restore function.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: BUN:Cr ratio helps distinguish prerenal from intrinsic renal injury at the bedside.

all_questions.question: Before thrombolytics in suspected stroke:
options: CT head to rule out hemorrhage
all_questions.rationale: Thrombolytics (tPA) are contraindicated in hemorrhagic stroke — they would worsen bleeding catastrophically. Non-contrast CT head is the emergent imaging study to rule out hemorrhage before thrombolytic consideration. Time is critical, but ruling out bleed takes priority over administering tPA.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Rule out hemorrhagic stroke before thrombolytics — CT is non-negotiable.

all_questions.question: Daily weight gain of 2 lb in CHF signals:
all_questions.rationale: Daily weight is the most sensitive early indicator of fluid retention in heart failure — 2 lb gain in 24 hours signals 1 liter of retained fluid. Patients should weigh daily at the same time; report gains of 2–3 lb in a day or 5 lb in a week. Catching fluid retention early prevents hospitalization for acute decompensated HF.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Daily weights catch fluid retention before crackles and dyspnea develop.

all_questions.question: DKA vs HHNS: ketones are prominent in:
all_questions.rationale: DKA features significant ketone production with metabolic acidosis (pH <7.3, anion gap elevated). HHS (hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state) presents with extreme hyperglycemia but minimal ketosis and less acidosis. Both are emergencies, but ketone prominence distinguishes DKA and guides insulin urgency.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Ketone presence differentiates DKA from HHS — both need treatment but pathways differ.

all_questions.question: ARDS P/F ratio Berlin definition uses PEEP:
all_questions.rationale: Berlin ARDS criteria require bilateral infiltrates, P/F ratio <300, and PEEP ≥5 cm H2O not fully explained by cardiac failure. ARDS severity is classified by P/F ratio: mild 200–300, moderate 100–200, severe <100. Lung-protective ventilation with low tidal volumes (6 mL/kg) is the cornerstone of management.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: ARDS requires lung-protective strategies — understand Berlin criteria for severity.

all_questions.question: HIT on heparin — priority action:
all_questions.rationale: Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) is an immune-mediated paradox: platelets drop but thrombosis risk soars. All heparin products must be stopped immediately when HIT is suspected — continuing causes arterial and venous clots. Alternative anticoagulation (argatroban, bivalirudin) is needed; platelet transfusion is contraindicated unless bleeding.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: HIT is a clotting disorder disguised as low platelets — stop heparin immediately.

all_questions.question: Which finding requires immediate notification?
options: SpO₂ 88% with RR 32 and accessory use
all_questions.rationale: SpO2 88% with respiratory rate 32 and accessory muscle use indicates acute respiratory distress requiring immediate action. The nurse must assess the airway, apply oxygen, position the patient, and notify the provider promptly. Mildly elevated BP, moderate pain, and normal temperature are not acute threats to airway or breathing.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Accessory muscle use with hypoxia signals respiratory failure — act on breathing before other findings.

all_questions.question: Best technique order for abdominal assessment?
all_questions.rationale: Abdominal assessment follows the order: inspect, auscultate, percuss, then palpate (IAPPP). Auscultation must occur before palpation because palpation can alter bowel sounds and lead to inaccurate findings. Palpating before auscultating is a common error that compromises assessment accuracy.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Assessment technique order prevents altering the very findings you need to detect.

all_questions.question: Orthostatic hypotension is defined as:
options: SBP drop ≥20 mmHg or DBP ≥10 mmHg with symptoms on standing
Any BP change lying to standing
all_questions.rationale: Orthostatic hypotension is defined as a drop in SBP ≥20 mmHg or DBP ≥10 mmHg within 3 minutes of standing, often with symptoms. This indicates inadequate cerebral perfusion on position change and increases fall risk. Any BP change or HR increase alone does not meet the clinical definition.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Orthostatic vital signs detect volume depletion and autonomic dysfunction before falls occur.

all_questions.question: FAST assessment includes:
all_questions.rationale: FAST is the stroke screening mnemonic: Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call emergency services. Rapid recognition and activation of the stroke team maximizes the window for thrombolytic therapy. Early identification directly impacts functional outcomes and mortality.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: FAST enables community and bedside stroke recognition — time activation saves brain tissue.

all_questions.question: Bowel sounds absent for 5 minutes in a post-op abdomen may indicate:
options: Ileus or obstruction — correlate with nausea, distension, flatus
all_questions.rationale: Absent bowel sounds after abdominal surgery may indicate postoperative ileus or developing obstruction. Correlate with nausea, abdominal distension, absence of flatus, and pain to differentiate expected ileus from complication. Notify the provider when bowel sounds remain absent with worsening distension or no flatus — obstruction can progress to ischemia.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Post-op abdominal assessment integrates bowel sounds with flatus, distension, and nausea.

all_questions.question: A positive Homans sign (calf pain on dorsiflexion) is:
options: Not reliable alone for DVT — use Wells criteria and ultrasound
all_questions.rationale: Homans sign (calf pain on dorsiflexion) is neither sensitive nor specific enough to diagnose DVT alone. Assess for unilateral calf swelling, warmth, erythema, and pain while applying Wells criteria and ordering ultrasound. Missed DVT is a leading cause of preventable pulmonary embolism in hospitalized patients.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Never rely on a single physical exam maneuver for DVT — use validated clinical decision tools.

all_questions.question: Late decelerations during labor — first action?
all_questions.rationale: Late decelerations indicate uteroplacental insufficiency — the fetus is not receiving adequate oxygen during contractions. Stop oxytocin if infusing, perform intrauterine resuscitation (position change, O2, IV fluids), and notify the provider. Increasing oxytocin or ignoring the monitor worsens fetal hypoxia and risks fetal demise.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Late decels = fetal hypoxia — intrauterine resuscitation is the nurse's immediate role.

all_questions.question: Postpartum fundus boggy — intervene with:
all_questions.rationale: A boggy, deviated uterus after delivery indicates uterine atony — the leading cause of postpartum hemorrhage. Fundal massage is first-line to stimulate contraction and reduce bleeding. Ice packs and bed rest do not address atony; prompt massage can prevent hemorrhagic shock.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: Boggy fundus = atony = hemorrhage risk — massage before medications when possible.

all_questions.question: Preeclampsia severe feature includes:
all_questions.rationale: RUQ/epigastric pain in preeclampsia suggests liver capsule distension and possible HELLP syndrome. This is a severe feature requiring immediate provider notification and likely magnesium sulfate and delivery planning. Mild ankle edema and Braxton Hicks contractions are common and not severe features.
all_questions.clinical_judgment_focus: RUQ pain in preeclampsia signals severe disease — do not dismiss as heartburn.
